segment_comparisons.finding: Undergraduates show more emotional/social ordering patterns, while postgraduates are more pragmatic and health-conscious
Shared accommodation drives more social ordering and kitchen avoidance, while solo living increases delivery frequency
Plus subscribers order more frequently but show guilt about increased spending, while non-subscribers are more price-sensitive
Heavy users normalize delivery as routine, while light users maintain it as special treat

patterns.pattern: Friday evening is the dominant delivery ordering time, positioned as end-of-week reward
Delivery ordering becomes more acceptable and less guilt-inducing in group contexts
Delivery triggers cluster around stress relief, reward, and comfort - particularly after exams or difficult days
Platform choice is primarily price-driven with minimal brand loyalty - participants actively compare across apps

say_do_gaps.interview_claim: Many participants claim to cook 'most meals' or order delivery 'once a week or less'
say_do_gaps.diary_reality: Only 46.7% of meals were home-cooked; 22.2% were skipped entirely. Actual delivery was 3.5% of all meal slots (11 orders across 315 meal opportunities)
say_do_gaps.implication: Participants significantly overestimate their cooking frequency and underestimate meal skipping

say_do_gaps.interview_claim: Participants describe consistent eating routines and cooking habits
say_do_gaps.diary_reality: Home cooking drops 30 percentage points on weekends (59% to 29%), meal skipping nearly doubles (17% to 32%)
say_do_gaps.implication: Weekend eating behavior is far more chaotic than participants acknowledge in interviews

say_do_gaps.interview_claim: Breakfast skipping mentioned casually as occasional behavior
say_do_gaps.diary_reality: 25% of all breakfasts were skipped; some participants (Rory C, Jill L) never ate breakfast during the entire 7-day period
say_do_gaps.implication: Breakfast skipping is a systematic habit, not occasional behavior, but participants don't frame it as problematic

say_do_gaps.interview_claim: Participants emphasize individual choice and personal triggers for ordering
say_do_gaps.diary_reality: Most delivery orders occurred in social contexts; weekend eating out spikes dramatically with 'socialising' as primary driver
say_do_gaps.implication: Social influence on food choices is stronger than participants consciously recognize

summary: Cross-participant analysis reveals stark differences between stated and actual eating behaviors. While participants claim consistent cooking routines, diary data shows only 46.7% of meals are home-cooked, with dramatic weekend disruption (cooking drops from 59% to 29%). Delivery apps represent just 3.5% of actual meal occasions despite extensive interview discussion, suggesting the topic looms larger in consciousness than behavior. Key segments differ predictably: undergraduates treat delivery as emotional reward (Friday treats, post-exam comfort), postgraduates are more health/budget pragmatic, and graduates show increased frequency but decreased specialness. Social context emerges as the strongest behavioral driver - delivery orders cluster around group situations, weekend socializing drives eating out, and even platform choice becomes collaborative price-comparison. The most significant finding is the normalization of meal skipping (22% of all meal slots) and breakfast avoidance (25% skip rate), which participants discuss casually but represents systematic disruption to eating patterns. This suggests delivery apps compete not just with home cooking, but with the decision to eat at all.
